Healthcare Team extension
Case study

Keeping a healthcare team on schedule when their designers vanished overnight

When Healio suddenly lost its design team mid-roadmap, we embedded senior designers within a week — and never let the plan slip.

01

An established product, suddenly without a team

Healio is a white-label healthcare platform that brings a person's scattered medical records together in one place, so they can actually monitor their own health.

As a mature company, Healio treats design as a competitive edge. Fresh off a redesign, they had a long backlog of new features queued up — and a clear plan from their design director to ship them over the coming months.

Then the plan broke. One in-house designer left for maternity leave; the other resigned. In a matter of days, a fully-staffed roadmap turned into a single director with no team to execute it.

Hiring senior designers in-house would take months Healio didn't have. They needed one to two senior UI/UX designers, full-time, able to start within a week.

02

Full-time designers, without the long hire

The advantage of working with us is simple: we can start the day after the first conversation. By the time most agencies have finished signing a contract, we already have two weeks of work behind us. We gave b.well two senior UI/UX designers — embedded as remote members of their product team, with the client in direct control and no project managers in between.

03

Working inside the product team

Once our designers knew the product and the people, they worked directly under the design director — picking up his plan and pushing it forward. Here are a few of the features we shipped together.

F1

Health Information Network — a sign-up people actually trust

HIN, designed from scratch, lets people find and connect medical records from different providers. Because that means handing over deeply private data, our north star was a single feeling: this is safe, and here's exactly why we're asking. The catch — three completely different authorization flows.

Connecting records, clearly

We designed each step to explain what's happening and why — choosing what to connect, watching the platform search on the user's behalf, and confirming when records come through.

  • Pre-verified users grant permission and watch a narrated search
  • HIPAA-protected users get records pulled automatically to confirm
  • Unauthorized users complete a two-factor ID scan and facial match
🔒 Designed to reassure at every step

We weren't sure how people would react to scanning IDs, so we prototyped the flow and ran user testing before committing. Few users dropped off during authorization — a strong signal the design worked.

F2

Health Summary — designed to ship in phases

Health Summary lets users compile a PDF of everything — visit history, diagnoses, lab results and more. b.well wanted it out fast, so developers started with the bare minimum: a download-only flow. We designed nine phases, each adding capability on top of the last.

Nine-phase rolloutDownload → full toolkit

Summary

We also rebuilt the PDF itself

The exported document was redesigned for clarity — readable hierarchy, breathing room, and a structure a patient or doctor can scan in seconds.

  • Clear typographic hierarchy replaced dense blocks
  • Grouped, scannable sections instead of raw dumps
  • Designed to be understood, not just produced
F3

Vitals — size, colour and white space

Vitals tracks blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen and more. The data was already there — it just wasn't readable. Grey containers smothered the content, hierarchy was flat, and nothing told users whether a number was good or worrying.

We killed the grey boxes

Working with the design lead, our most important call was the simplest: remove the grey containers and let white space do the work. We built a type system, fixed the hierarchy, and the section instantly felt airier and easier to read.

Then, to make health legible at a glance, we introduced five clear indicator levels — each with its own colour and icon.

Low
Below safe
Below range
Watch
In range
Healthy
Above range
Watch
High
Above safe

b.well's executives were impressed enough by the new look that they asked for the whole platform to adopt the style.

F4

Labs — from a wall of dates to a tool you can search

The Labs page holds every test result, old and new. Originally everything was grouped by date with no search or filter, so finding anything was a slog. Our goal: make Labs genuinely useful for health monitoring.

Iterated until it was clear

  • Made each result its own item in the list
  • Removed the grey blocks — same as Vitals
  • Added an indicator for quick result preview
  • Introduced search and filter

On the Lab Detail page we upgraded the chart so people can track a single result over time — choose a timeframe, and see a healthy range plotted against their numbers.

F5

Provider search — no wrong doctor by mistake

The provider database is enormous, and duplicate names are common. We designed search so users could never accidentally connect to the wrong doctor.

Make the right choice obvious

In the Search Connections by Provider card, we made each doctor's office location prominent and tappable — so people can confirm they've found the right person. We also added a “Request Connection” action, letting users ask to join a doctor's health system if they aren't in it yet.

The outcome

Back on track — with insurance against the unexpected

Our designers slotted into the in-house team so cleanly that Healio kept pace with their roadmap throughout. When the original designer returned from maternity leave, we worked alongside her. Three months in, Healio hired another in-house designer and our role wrapped — and when that hire couldn't start on time, they asked us back.
